front: Flink vs Kafka Streams: how do they compare on statefulness and when does each win?
back: Both support stateful streaming, but their architecture and operational models differ fundamentally. Kafka Streams stores state in local RocksDB instances backed by Kafka changelog topics for fault tolerance — no external state backend required. This makes it self-contained and operationally simple. Flink uses pluggable state backends (heap or RocksDB) backed by distributed checkpoints to HDFS/S3, requiring separate storage infrastructure. Kafka Streams wins for: simplicity (it's a library, no cluster to manage), Kafka-native deployments, microservice architectures, and moderate throughput. Flink wins for: complex event processing across multiple streams, very large state (terabytes), SQL/Table API analytics, exactly-once with non-Kafka sinks, advanced windowing with custom triggers, and when you need sub-second latency at very high scale. Key limitation of Kafka Streams: it only reads from/writes to Kafka — Flink can integrate with virtually any source/sink.
